items.title: Latin Root Words: 10 High-Frequency Exam Words Decoded by Their Roots
items.summary: Ten high-frequency exam words built on Latin roots, each with its root, meaning, an original usage sentence, and close synonyms to help you decode unfamiliar vocabulary quickly.
items.body:
Many of the toughest words in exam word lists descend from a handful of Latin roots. Once you can spot the root inside an unfamiliar word, you can often decode its meaning without a dictionary. Learn the ten entries below along with their roots and revise them as a family.

Abrogate (from rogare, to propose) — to repeal or abolish a law, treaty, or custom formally. Usage: Parliament voted to abrogate the colonial-era statute. Synonyms: annul, revoke, rescind.

Benevolent (bene, well) — kindly, well-meaning, and generous in spirit. Usage: The benevolent trustee quietly funded scholarships for village students. Synonyms: kind, charitable, magnanimous.

Credulous (credere, to believe) — too ready to believe things; easily deceived. Usage: Credulous investors poured their savings into the dubious scheme. Synonyms: gullible, naive, unsuspecting.

Dictum (dicere, to say) — a formal pronouncement or authoritative saying. Usage: The judge's dictum on privacy guided several later rulings. Synonyms: pronouncement, maxim, decree.

Fidelity (fides, faith) — faithfulness to a person, cause, or original source. Usage: The translation shows remarkable fidelity to the original poem. Synonyms: loyalty, allegiance, faithfulness.

Magnanimous (magnus, great, plus animus, mind) — generous and forgiving, especially toward a rival. Usage: The champion was magnanimous in victory and praised her opponent warmly. Synonyms: generous, noble, big-hearted.

Pacify (pax, peace) — to calm anger or agitation; to restore peace. Usage: The manager tried to pacify the frustrated customers with a quick refund. Synonyms: appease, placate, mollify.

Tenacious (tenere, to hold) — holding firmly to a purpose; persistent. Usage: Her tenacious preparation through two failed attempts finally earned her the seat. Synonyms: persistent, dogged, resolute.

Verity (verus, true) — a true principle or belief; truthfulness. Usage: The essay questioned the verity of several popular historical legends. Synonyms: truth, fact, authenticity.

Circumspect (circum, around, plus specere, to look) — wary and careful; weighing all consequences before acting. Usage: Be circumspect before signing any long-term financial contract. Synonyms: cautious, prudent, vigilant.

Revision tip: group new words by root rather than alphabetically. One root such as tenere also unlocks tenure, tenable, retentive, and pertinacious, so a single root can earn you five or six words at once.

items.title: Greek Root Words: 10 Exam Words Built on Greek Roots
items.summary: Ten exam-relevant words derived from Greek roots, each broken down into its root, meaning, an original usage sentence, and close synonyms for faster decoding in reading passages.
items.body:
Greek roots power much of English academic and editorial vocabulary. Words about time, feeling, speech, and people very often trace back to Greek. Master the ten entries below and you will start decoding unseen words in reading comprehension passages with ease.

Anachronism (chronos, time) — something placed in the wrong historical period. Usage: A wristwatch on the medieval king was an obvious anachronism in the film. Synonyms: chronological error, incongruity, misplacement.

Apathy (pathos, feeling) — absence of interest, enthusiasm, or concern. Usage: Voter apathy kept turnout low in the municipal elections. Synonyms: indifference, unconcern, lethargy.

Cryptic (kryptos, hidden) — having a hidden or puzzling meaning. Usage: His cryptic reply left the panel more curious than satisfied. Synonyms: enigmatic, obscure, mysterious.

Demagogue (demos, people, plus agogos, leader) — a leader who wins support by stirring emotions rather than offering reasoned argument. Usage: The novel portrays a demagogue who thrives on public fear. Synonyms: rabble-rouser, agitator, firebrand.

Ephemeral (hemera, day) — lasting a very short time. Usage: Online fame is often ephemeral, fading within weeks. Synonyms: fleeting, transient, short-lived.

Euphemism (eu, good, plus pheme, speech) — a mild expression substituted for a blunt or harsh one. Usage: "Let go" is a common euphemism for being dismissed from a job. Synonyms: polite expression, indirect term, softening.

Misanthrope (misein, to hate, plus anthropos, human) — a person who dislikes and avoids other people. Usage: The reclusive misanthrope skipped every neighbourhood gathering. Synonyms: cynic, recluse, hermit.

Neologism (neos, new, plus logos, word) — a newly coined word or expression. Usage: Every wave of technology adds a fresh neologism to the dictionary. Synonyms: coinage, new term, invented word.

Polyglot (poly, many, plus glotta, tongue) — a person who speaks several languages. Usage: A natural polyglot, she switched effortlessly among four languages during the tour. Synonyms: multilingual speaker, linguist.

Sycophant (from sykophantes) — a person who flatters the powerful for personal gain. Usage: The director surrounded himself with sycophants who never disagreed with him. Synonyms: flatterer, toady, bootlicker.

Revision tip: whenever you meet a new word containing chron, path, log, or anthrop, pause and guess its meaning from the root before checking. Active guessing fixes roots in memory far better than passive reading.

items.title: Synonym Clusters for Editorial Reading: 10 Words That Dominate Opinion Pages
items.summary: Ten formal words that dominate editorial prose, presented as synonym clusters with meanings and original usage sentences so you can read opinion pieces faster and more accurately.
items.body:
Editorial writers draw on a compact pool of formal verbs and adjectives, and the same ideas reappear in different clothes every day. If you learn these words in clusters, recognising any one member of the cluster lets you understand all the others. Here are ten anchors with their families.

Mitigate — to make something less severe or harmful. Usage: Early warning systems mitigate the damage caused by cyclones. Synonyms: alleviate, lessen, temper, soften.

Exacerbate — to make a bad situation worse. Usage: Fuel shortages exacerbated the city's transport crisis. Synonyms: aggravate, worsen, intensify, inflame.

Bolster — to support, strengthen, or reinforce. Usage: Fresh investment bolstered the struggling manufacturing sector. Synonyms: buttress, fortify, reinforce, shore up.

Undermine — to weaken something gradually, often quietly. Usage: Constant interference undermines the independence of regulators. Synonyms: erode, sap, subvert, weaken.

Stringent — strict, precise, and demanding. Usage: The regulator imposed stringent disclosure requirements on lenders. Synonyms: rigorous, severe, exacting, tough.

Pragmatic — dealing with matters practically rather than ideologically. Usage: A pragmatic policy balances growth with fiscal discipline. Synonyms: practical, realistic, sensible, hard-headed.

Contentious — causing or likely to cause disagreement. Usage: Land acquisition remains a contentious issue in infrastructure debates. Synonyms: controversial, disputed, divisive, debatable.

Prudent — acting with care and thought for the future. Usage: Prudent households build an emergency fund before chasing returns. Synonyms: judicious, cautious, sensible, circumspect.

Ubiquitous — present or found everywhere. Usage: Digital payments have become ubiquitous in urban markets. Synonyms: omnipresent, pervasive, universal, widespread.

Ambivalent — holding mixed or contradictory feelings about something. Usage: Economists remain ambivalent about the merits of the new proposal. Synonyms: undecided, equivocal, conflicted, torn.

Practice method: pick any opinion piece, underline every word from these clusters, and mentally swap it with a synonym from the same family. If the sentence still reads naturally, you have truly absorbed the cluster rather than memorised an isolated definition.

items.title: Antonym Pairs: 10 Words with Meanings, Synonyms, and Sharp Opposites
items.summary: Ten word entries with meanings, original usage sentences, close synonyms, and a sharp antonym for each, built for the antonym questions that appear across SSC and Banking papers.
items.body:
Antonym questions reward students who learn words in opposing pairs rather than in isolation. Each entry below gives a meaning, an original sentence, close synonyms, and one sharp antonym you can recall instantly in the exam hall.

Candid — frank and honest in expression. Usage: Her candid feedback improved the final draft considerably. Synonyms: frank, forthright, open. Antonym: evasive.

Frugal — economical; careful to avoid waste. Usage: A frugal lifestyle helped him repay the education loan early. Synonyms: thrifty, economical, sparing. Antonym: extravagant.

Obscure — not clearly expressed or not well known. Usage: The poet remained obscure during his own lifetime. Synonyms: unclear, unknown, abstruse. Antonym: renowned.

Transient — lasting only a short time. Usage: The town hosts a transient population of seasonal workers. Synonyms: temporary, fleeting, ephemeral. Antonym: permanent.

Hostile — unfriendly or antagonistic. Usage: The takeover bid met a hostile response from the board. Synonyms: antagonistic, unfriendly, belligerent. Antonym: amicable.

Apathetic — showing no interest or emotion. Usage: Apathetic residents ignored the cleanliness drive in the colony. Synonyms: indifferent, unconcerned, listless. Antonym: enthusiastic.

Scarce — insufficient; in short supply. Usage: Skilled welders are scarce in the region's shipyards. Synonyms: rare, meagre, sparse. Antonym: abundant.

Rigid — inflexible; unwilling to bend or change. Usage: Rigid procedures discouraged creative solutions in the office. Synonyms: inflexible, stiff, unbending. Antonym: flexible.

Humble — modest about one's own importance. Usage: Despite international fame, the scientist stayed humble. Synonyms: modest, unassuming, meek. Antonym: arrogant.

Verbose — using more words than necessary. Usage: The verbose report buried its key finding under jargon. Synonyms: wordy, long-winded, prolix. Antonym: laconic.

Memory technique: write each pair on opposite ends of one line, such as frugal versus extravagant, and revise the line as a single unit. When the exam asks for the opposite of either word, the partner surfaces automatically.

items.title: Idioms and Phrases: 10 Expressions Every Aspirant Should Master
items.summary: Ten everyday idioms with plain meanings, original usage sentences, and similar expressions, covering the idiom questions that appear in SSC and Banking English sections.
items.body:
Idioms carry meanings that cannot be guessed word by word, which is exactly why examiners love them. The ten expressions below appear regularly in English sections, in cloze tests, and in everyday professional conversation.

A blessing in disguise — something that seems unfortunate at first but turns out to be beneficial. Usage: Missing that train was a blessing in disguise, because I met my future mentor on the next one. Synonyms: hidden benefit, silver lining.

Bite the bullet — to face a painful or unpleasant situation with courage. Usage: She bit the bullet and admitted the billing error to her supervisor. Synonyms: face the music, grit your teeth.

Burn the midnight oil — to work or study late into the night. Usage: Serious aspirants burn the midnight oil in the final weeks before the exam. Synonyms: work late, toil into the night.

Cut corners — to do a task cheaply or carelessly by skipping necessary steps. Usage: The builder cut corners on materials, and the walls cracked within a year. Synonyms: skimp, compromise on quality.

Hit the nail on the head — to describe a situation or problem exactly. Usage: Her diagnosis of the team's communication gap hit the nail on the head. Synonyms: be spot on, get it exactly right.

Let the cat out of the bag — to reveal a secret, usually by accident. Usage: He let the cat out of the bag about the surprise farewell party. Synonyms: spill the beans, give the game away.

Once in a blue moon — very rarely. Usage: My cousin lives abroad and visits us once in a blue moon. Synonyms: rarely, hardly ever.

The ball is in your court — it is now your turn to act or decide. Usage: We have sent the revised offer, so the ball is in their court. Synonyms: your move, your call.

Throw in the towel — to give up or admit defeat. Usage: After three failed attempts, he still refused to throw in the towel. Synonyms: surrender, concede defeat.

Under the weather — slightly unwell. Usage: She skipped morning practice because she was feeling under the weather. Synonyms: unwell, out of sorts.

Usage tip: idioms are fixed expressions; never change their wording. Writing "the ball is in your field" or "burn the night oil" turns a correct answer into a wrong one.

items.title: One-Word Substitutions: 10 Classic Entries for Quick Scoring
items.summary: Ten classic one-word substitutions, from altruist to teetotaller, each with its meaning, an original usage sentence, and synonyms for quick marks in SSC and Banking vocabulary sections.
items.body:
One-word substitution questions ask you to replace a long phrase with a single precise word. They are among the fastest marks in any paper if the words below are at your fingertips.

Altruist — a person who selflessly works for the welfare of others. Usage: A genuine altruist, she donated her entire prize money to the shelter. Synonyms: philanthropist, humanitarian.

Bibliophile — a person who loves or collects books. Usage: The bibliophile spent every Sunday hunting for rare first editions. Synonyms: book lover, book collector.

Connoisseur — an expert judge in matters of taste such as art, food, or music. Usage: Only a connoisseur could tell the two vintages apart. Synonyms: expert, aficionado.

Insolvent — a person or firm unable to pay its debts. Usage: The court declared the airline insolvent after years of mounting losses. Synonyms: bankrupt, financially ruined.

Omniscient — knowing everything. Usage: The novel uses an omniscient narrator who can enter every character's mind. Synonyms: all-knowing, all-seeing.

Posthumous — happening or awarded after a person's death. Usage: The soldier received a posthumous gallantry award. Synonyms: after-death (used attributively), post-mortem in limited contexts.

Teetotaller — a person who never drinks alcohol. Usage: A lifelong teetotaller, he raised a glass of juice at the celebration. Synonyms: abstainer, non-drinker.

Gregarious — fond of company; sociable. Usage: Her gregarious nature made her the natural host of every office gathering. Synonyms: sociable, outgoing, convivial.

Indelible — impossible to remove, erase, or forget. Usage: The mentor left an indelible mark on a generation of students. Synonyms: permanent, unforgettable, enduring.

Nostalgia — a sentimental longing for the past. Usage: The old school photograph filled him with sudden nostalgia. Synonyms: wistfulness, longing, reminiscence.

Exam strategy: read the given phrase, isolate its key idea — a person, a quality, or an event — and match that idea to the right word class. Phrases beginning with "one who" almost always point to a person word such as altruist, bibliophile, or teetotaller.

items.title: Commonly Confused Word Pairs: Affect/Effect and 9 More Clarified
items.summary: Ten commonly confused word pairs such as affect/effect and eminent/imminent, clarified with meanings, original usage sentences, and synonyms for each member of the pair.
items.body:
Confusable pairs sound or look alike but differ sharply in meaning, and a single slip can cost an easy mark. Study the ten pairs below until the difference feels automatic.

Affect vs Effect — Affect is usually a verb meaning to influence; effect is usually a noun meaning a result. Usage: The strike affected supplies, and its effect lasted a month. Synonyms: affect — influence, alter; effect — result, consequence.

Adapt vs Adopt — Adapt means to adjust to new conditions; adopt means to take up or accept something. Usage: Farmers adapt to erratic rainfall, and many adopt drip irrigation. Synonyms: adapt — adjust, modify; adopt — embrace, take up.

Eminent vs Imminent — Eminent means distinguished; imminent means about to happen. Usage: The eminent scientist warned that a water crisis was imminent. Synonyms: eminent — renowned, distinguished; imminent — impending, looming.

Stationary vs Stationery — Stationary means not moving; stationery means writing materials. Usage: The van stood stationary outside the shop that sold stationery. Synonyms: stationary — motionless, static; stationery — writing supplies.

Principal vs Principle — Principal means chief, or the head of an institution; principle means a rule or belief. Usage: The principal reminded students of the principle of honesty. Synonyms: principal — chief, main; principle — doctrine, tenet.

Allusion vs Illusion — An allusion is an indirect reference; an illusion is a false perception. Usage: Her essay made an allusion to the epic, while the desert mirage was an optical illusion. Synonyms: allusion — reference, hint; illusion — delusion, mirage.

Complement vs Compliment — Complement means to complete or enhance; compliment means an expression of praise. Usage: The scarf complements her outfit, and she received many compliments. Synonyms: complement — complete, enhance; compliment — praise, flattery.

Elicit vs Illicit — Elicit is a verb meaning to draw out; illicit is an adjective meaning unlawful. Usage: The inquiry elicited details about the illicit mining operation. Synonyms: elicit — extract, evoke; illicit — illegal, unlawful.

Lose vs Loose — Lose is a verb meaning to misplace or fail to win; loose is an adjective meaning not tight. Usage: Tighten the loose strap or you will lose the bag. Synonyms: lose — misplace, forfeit; loose — slack, unfastened.

Ingenious vs Ingenuous — Ingenious means cleverly inventive; ingenuous means innocent and frank. Usage: His ingenious fix saved the bridge design, while her ingenuous answer disarmed the panel. Synonyms: ingenious — inventive, resourceful; ingenuous — naive, candid.
